critic_raw:
The most fundamental flaw in this design is applying a stratified random split that ensures fault examples appear in the autoencoder's training set, directly violating the core assumption of autoencoder-based anomaly detection. Autoencoders detect anomalies by reconstructing inputs and flagging high reconstruction error — this works only if the autoencoder was trained exclusively on normal (fault-free) data. When fault examples are included in training, the autoencoder learns to reconstruct them too, collapsing the reconstruction error gap between normal and faulty states. The stratified split is specifically designed to include faults in training, which actively sabotages the anomaly detection mechanism.

Mean time to failure (MTTF) as the primary evaluation metric requires observing complete run-to-failure trajectories. A stratified random split fragments these trajectories across splits — sensor readings leading up to a failure event may be split between training, validation, and test sets. MTTF computed on fragmented test-set trajectories is not the MTTF under the model's deployed behavior; it is an artifact of which trajectory segments happened to land in the test set.

The comparison between an autoencoder and a threshold-based baseline is not a fair comparison of detection capability when the autoencoder has been trained on fault data. The threshold-based baseline effectively uses a similar unsupervised approach (deviation from expected range), but the autoencoder with mixed training has a fundamentally different (and compromised) signal structure.

For an unsupervised method, 'precision at top-K anomalies' as a secondary metric requires ground truth fault labels during evaluation — yet the method is framed as unsupervised precisely because such labels are assumed unavailable or unreliable. The design uses fault labels for evaluation but claims unsupervised detection capability, without addressing this contradiction.
all_issues_raised: The stratified random split ensures fault examples appear in the autoencoder's training data, directly destroying the reconstruction-error-based anomaly detection mechanism — autoencoders trained on mixed normal-and-fault data learn to reconstruct faults, collapsing the detection boundary.

MTTF computation on a stratified random split produces fragmented run-to-failure trajectories — MTTF measured on test-set trajectory fragments is not the MTTF under the deployed policy and reflects split artifact rather than detection capability.

Precision at top-K anomalies requires fault labels during evaluation, contradicting the framing of the method as unsupervised — the design implicitly uses supervised evaluation while claiming unsupervised generalization, conflating two different problem settings.

Training the autoencoder on mixed fault and normal data while comparing it to a threshold-based system creates an asymmetric comparison where the autoencoder's mechanism is compromised but the threshold baseline operates normally, biasing the comparison in favor of the baseline.
all_issues_adjudicated: The stratified random split ensures fault examples appear in the autoencoder's training data, directly destroying the reconstruction-error-based anomaly detection mechanism — autoencoders trained on mixed normal-and-fault data learn to reconstruct faults, collapsing the detection boundary.

MTTF computation on a stratified random split produces fragmented run-to-failure trajectories — MTTF measured on test-set trajectory fragments is not the MTTF under the deployed policy and reflects split artifact rather than detection capability.

Precision at top-K anomalies requires fault labels during evaluation, contradicting the framing of the method as unsupervised — the design implicitly uses supervised evaluation while claiming unsupervised generalization, conflating two different problem settings.

Training the autoencoder on mixed fault and normal data while comparing it to a threshold-based system creates an asymmetric comparison where the autoencoder's mechanism is compromised but the threshold baseline operates normally, biasing the comparison in favor of the baseline.
